titleStmt.title: Spectra and pseudo-spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices and the topological origin of the non-Hermitian skin effect

analytic.title: Spectra and pseudo-spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices and the topological origin of the non-Hermitian skin effect

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We establish new results on the spectra and pseudo-spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators and matrices. In particular, we prove the connection between the winding number of the eigenvalues of the symbol function and the exponential decay of the associated eigenvectors (or pseudo-eigenvectors). Our results elucidate the topological origin of the non-Hermitian skin effect in general one-dimensional polymer systems of subwavelength resonators with imaginary gauge potentials, proving the observation and conjecture in [5]. We also numerically verify our theory for these systems.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Motivated by the study of the non-Hermitian skin effect in polymer systems of subwavelength resonators and its topological origin, in this paper we extend the theory on the spectra and pseudo-spectra of Toeplitz operators and matrices to tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators and matrices. Our main goal is to establish a connection between, on one hand, the winding number of the eigenvalues of the symbol function of a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator and, on the other hand, the exponential decay of the associated eigenvectors or pseudo-eigenvectors. By doing so, we elucidate the topological origin of the eigenmode condensation at one edge of a polymer system of subwavelength resonators where an imaginary gauge potential is added inside the resonators to break Hermiticity. We show that, due to a nonzero total winding of the eigenvalues of the symbol of the k-Toeplitz operator corresponding to the polymer system, the eigenmodes exhibit exponential decay and condensate at one of the edges of the structure.

In addition to advancing our understanding of the non-Hermitian skin effect, which can be seen as the classical analogue of the non-Hermitian Anderson model [16], one of our key contributions in this paper is to optimally characterize the spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators. Notably, we reveal the distinctive property that the eigenvectors of these operators undergo exponential decay. Using the notion of pseudo-spectrum, we rigorously justify the exponential decay of the pseudo-eigenvectors of the associated k-Toeplitz matrices and significantly generalize the results presented in [9,15,18,20,21].

Our paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we first provide an optimal characterization of the spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz and Laurent operators. Then we prove the existence of an exponentially decaying eigenvector for Topelitz operators. In Section 3, we establish a connection between the eigenvectors of a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator and the associated tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrix. In Section 4 we discuss the topological origin of the non-Hermitian skin effect in polymer systems of subwavelength resonators and numerically verify our findings on the spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators. In Section 5, we make some concluding remarks and give some possible extensions of our present work.

div.head: Spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators
div.p: In this section, we characterize the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators and prove that their associated eigenvectors exhibit exponential decay. This type of operators and their associated truncated operators, i.e. matrices, are crucial in the study of the one-dimensional polymer systems of subwavelength resonators [1,5,6,11]. We define a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator by
where a i , b i , c i ∈ C for i ∈ N and A ij = 0 if |i -j| > 1 for i, j ∈ N. Its finite, truncated, counterpart will be called a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrix.

To characterize the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators, we introduce the notions of Fredholm operator and Fredholm index. Let X be a Banach space and B(X) the set of bounded linear operators on X. We say that an operator
div.formula: A ∈ B(X) is a Fredholm operator if Im A is a closed subspace in X and dim Ker(A) < ∞, dim CokerA < ∞,
div.p: where CokerA := X/ImA. The index of the Fredholm operator A is defined as

div.head: Tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators
div.p: Observe that the tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator A presented in (2.1) can be reformulated as a tridiagonal block Toeplitz operator, where the blocks repeat in a 1-periodic way:
with
The symbol of the tridiagonal block Toeplitz operator (2.3) (and thus also of the tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator (2.1)) is defined by
or explicitly
We will write A = T (f ). In the following sections, we will characterize the spectrum of the operator T (f ) based on its symbol f (z).

div.head: Hardy Spaces
div.p: To get a better understanding of the connection between the symbol f and the operator T (f ) itself, it is necessary to introduce the so-called Hardy Spaces. Let T be the unit circle in C.
div.formula: H 2 := {f ∈ L 2 (T) : f n = 0 for n < 0}, H 2 -:= {f ∈ L 2 (T) : f n = 0 for n ≥ 0}
div.p: , where f n denotes the Fourier coefficients of f . The spaces H 2 and H 2 -are closed, orthogonal subspaces of L 2 := L 2 (T). Hence, one may write
div.formula: L 2 = H 2 ⊕ H 2 -. Denote by P the projection of L 2 onto H 2 . The functions 1 √ 2π e inθ ∞ n=0
div.p: form an orthonormal basis of H 2 . One important property of functions in H 2 is given by the following theorem [10,Theorem 6.13].
Theorem 2.2 (F. and M. Riesz). If f is a nonzero function in H 2 , then the set {e it ∈ T : f (e it ) = 0} has measure zero.
It is well-known [21] that for a Toeplitz operator T (f ) with symbol f ∈ L ∞ , T (f ) is the matrix representation of the operator

in the above orthonormal basis. A similar notion holds for block Toeplitz operators. We denote by (H 2 ) k the k-dimensional vector space whose entries are elements of H 2 . If P is the orthogonal projection of (L 2 ) k onto (H 2 ) k and f ∈ (L ∞ ) k×k , then T (f ) is the matrix representation of the operator

where f g is the usual matrix-vector multiplication. Note that the (H 2 ) k equivalent of Theorem 2.2 holds verbatim with H 2 replaced by (H 2 ) k . With this in mind, we are now able to prove a generalization of Coburn's lemma (for the Toeplitz case, see [10,Theorem 1.10]). Theorem 2.3 (Coburn's lemma; tridiagonal k-Toeplitz version). Let f ∈ C k×k (T) be the symbol of a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator such that det(f (z)) does not vanish identically on T. Then one of the following statements holds:
(i) T (f ) has a trivial kernel;
(ii) T (f ) has a dense range;
(iii) The leading (k -1) × (k -1) principal minor of A 0 has a nonzero kernel. In particular, there exists some z 0 ∈ C ∪{∞} such that ker(z

Proof. It is not hard to see that the adjoint of T (f ) is T (f ⊤ ), where the superscript ⊤ denotes the transpose. Assume that T (f ) has a nontrivial kernel and a nondense range, i.e., conditions (i) and (ii) do not hold. This implies that T (f ⊤ ) has a nontrivial kernel as well.
Hence there exist nonzero functions g + , h + ∈ (H 2 ) k such that
where
div.formula: g ± = (g ±,1 , g ±,2 , • • • , g ±,k ) ⊤ and h ± = (h ±,1 , h ±,2 , • • • , h ±,k ) ⊤ . Since g + , h + ∈ (H 2 ) k , there exist two sequences of vectors (v n ) n , (w n ) n ∈ C k such that g + = n∈N v n z n-1 , h + = n∈N w n z n-1 ,(2.7)
div.p: for z = e iθ , where we have absorbed the factor 1 √ 2π into v n and w n . This allows us to write
where we have used that g -∈ (H 2 -) k in the last equality. Similarly, using that h -∈ (H 2 -) k , we obtain the following expression for h -:
(2.9) By Theorem 2.2, it follows that g + , h + ̸ = 0 almost everywhere on T. We define
It holds that φ ∈ L 1 . Moreover, φ n = (h ⊤ -g + ) n = 0 for n ≤ 0 and φ n = (h ⊤ + g -) n = 0 for n ≥ 0 since by (2.7), (2.8), and (2.9),
div.formula: h ⊤ + g -= n∈N z -n w ⊤ n A -1 v 1 , h ⊤ -g + = n∈N z n (A 1 ⊤ w 1 ) ⊤ v n .
div.p: Hence φ = 0. This implies
div.formula: A 1 ⊤ w 1 ⊥ v n and A -1 v 1 ⊥ w n for n ∈ N. Thus either v n ⊥ e k , n = 1, • • • , or w 1 ∈ ker A 1 ⊤ . However, if w 1 ∈ ker A 1 ⊤
div.p: , then by (2.9)

which contradicts the fact that det(f ⊤ ) = det(f ) does not vanish identically on T. Therefore, v n ⊥ e k for n ∈ N ≥1 . By the above arguments, we conclude that if T (f ) has non-dense range and nontrivial kernel, then v n ⊥ e k for n ∈ N ≥1 and for any v n defined by (2.7). Now we prove that if T (f ) has a non-dense range and a nontrivial kernel, then the condition (iii) must hold. Since
div.formula: f g + = g -∈ (H 2 -) k , by expansion (2.8) it holds that A -1 v n+2 + A 0 v n+1 + A 1 v n = 0 for n ∈ N. Since v n ⊥ e k for n ∈ N ≥1 it follows that v n ∈ ker(A 1 ). Hence it holds that A -1 v n+1 + A 0 v n = 0 for n ∈ N ≥2 . Note that the expansion (2.8) also gives A -1 v 2 + A 0 v 1 = 0. Therefore, A -1 v n+1 + A 0 v n = 0 for n ∈ N ≥1 . Furthermore, since the image of A -1 is confined to the span of e k and v n ⊥ e k for n ∈ N ≥1 , we have A 0 | (k-1)×(k-1) v n | 1:k-1 = 0, where A 0 | (k-1)×(k-1) is the leading principal (k -1) × (k -1) submatrix of A 0 . This also means that A 0 | (k-1)×(k-1)
div.p: cannot have full rank. On the other hand, one can compute that
where v
n is the j-th element of v n . Considering the case when n = 1, by (2.8), we know that v
div.formula: 1 ̸ = 0, otherwise, f g + = g -= z -1 A -1 v 1 =
div.p: 0, which contradicts det f does not vanish identically on T. Similarly, we can also show that b k ̸ = 0. Thus by (2.10) and b k ̸ = 0 there exists some z 0 ∈ C ∪{∞} such that v 1 lies in the kernel of z -1 0 A -1 + A 0 . Therefore it holds that ker(z -1 0 A -1 + A 0 ) ∩ ker(A 1 ) ̸ = 0, which concludes the proof. ■

div.head: Spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators
div.p: In this section, we will provide a full characterization of the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators. We begin by first recalling (and generalizing) a few theorems on Toeplitz operators. The first important theorem is a consequence of [ We denote by σ(•), σ ess (•) the spectrum and the essential spectrum of the operator, respectively. We define
We first have the following theorem characterizing the essential spectrum of T (f ).
Theorem 2.4. The operator T (f ) in (2.3) is Fredholm if and only if det(f ) has no zeros on T. Furthermore, the essential spectrum of T (f ) is given by

Proof. The first part has been proven in [15,Chapter 23,Theorem 4.3]. For the second part, note that λ ∈ σ ess (T (f )) if and only if T (f )-λ is not a Fredholm operator. By the first part, this happens only if det(f (z 0 ) -λ) = 0 for some z 0 ∈ T. That is, λ is an eigenvalue of f (z 0 ). On the other hand, if λ is an eigenvalue for some z 0 ∈ T, it holds that det(f (z 0 ) -λ) = 0, which concludes the proof. ■
We now recall the following Theorem due to Gohberg [15, Chapter 23, Theorem 5.1].
div.formula: Theorem 2.5. Let f : T → C k×k be such that T (f ) is a Toeplitz operator. Then, T (f ) is Fredholm on the space ℓ 2 if

div.head: and only if det(f ) has no zeros on T, in which case
div.p: Ind T (f ) = -wind(det(f (T)), 0), with wind(det(f (T)), 0) being the winding number about the origin of the determinant of f (z) with z ∈ T.

Utilizing this theorem together with Theorem 2.3, we are now able to establish one of the main results of our paper, an optimal characterization of the spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators. Before stating the theorem, we first define
div.formula: σ wind (f ) := {λ ∈ C \ σ det (f ) : wind(det(f (T) -λ), 0) ̸ = 0}.
By Lemma A.1, det(f (z) -λ) = (-1) k+1 k i=1 c i z + (-1) k+1 k i=1 b i z -1 + g(λ)
div.p: with g(λ) being given by (A.2), which yields
div.formula: σ wind (f ) = λ ∈ C \ σ det (f ) : wind (-1) k+1 ( k i=1 c i )z + ( k i=1 b i )z -1 , -g(λ) ̸ = 0 . (2.13) Furthermore, since det(f (z) -λ) = k j=1 (λ j (z) -λ),
div.p: where λ j (z), 1 ≤ j ≤ k, are the eigenvalues of the matrix f (z), we obtain
div.formula: σ wind (f ) =    λ ∈ C \ σ det (f ) : k j=1 wind(λ j (T) -λ, 0) ̸ = 0    =    λ ∈ C \ σ det (f ) : k j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) ̸ = 0    .
div.p: Note that the representations (2.12), (2.13) and (2.14) have their own pro and contra. For relating our results to the conjecture in [5], we utilize representation (2.14) in our main results. We now establish the following theorem for the spectrum of the operator T (f ). Theorem 2.6. Let f ∈ C k×k (T) be the symbol of a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator T (f ). Denote by B 0 the leading (k -1) × (k -1) principal minor of A 0 . It holds that
div.formula: σ det (f ) ∪ σ wind (f ) ⊂ σ(T (f )) ⊂ σ det (f ) ∪ σ wind (f ) ∪ σ(B 0 ),
div.p: where σ det (f ), σ wind (f ) are given by (2.11), (2.14), respectively.
Proof. The first inclusion is an immediate consequence of Theorems 2.4 and 2.5. For the second inclusion, note that if
then it must hold that T (f ) -λI is Fredholm and Ind(T (f
div.formula: ) -λI) = wind(det(f -λ), 0) = 0. Based on the definition (2.2), this implies dim Ker(T (f ) -λI) = dim(ℓ 2 \ Im(T (f ) -λI)). (2.16)
div.p: Moreover, since λ ∈ σ(T (f )), T (f ) -λI has a non-trivial kernel and non-dense image. By Theorem 2.3, this implies that λ must be an eigenvalue of B 0 . ■ This characterization is optimal. It cannot be made more precise, as it cannot be guaranteed that an eigenvalue λ of B 0 with wind(det(f -λ), 0) = 0 is also an eigenvalue of T (f ). To illustrate this, consider
The determinant of this symbol is given by det

). We have wind(det(f (T)), 0) = 0 and det f (z) ̸ = 0, ∀z ∈ T. In this case, B 0 = 0 is just the top left entry of f (z). Furthermore, by (2.2), if 0 ∈ σ(T (f )), then the kernel space of T (f ) must be non-trivial. Since

, one can check that, after scaling, the only possible eigenvector of T (f ) corresponding to the eigenvalue 0 is u = (1, 0, -2, 0, 4, . . . ) ⊤ . However, u does not lie in ℓ 2 , which means that 0 ̸ ∈ σ(T (f )).
On the other hand, consider

The determinant is det( f ) = -(1+2z)(1+2z -1 ), hence 0 / ∈ σ det (f ) and wind(det( f (T)), 0) = 0. Therefore, by Theorem 2.3, the operator T ( f ) is either invertible or has a nontrivial kernel. By explicitly writing down T ( f )

Remark 2.7. Note that (2.13) and (2.14) also provide ways to compute σ wind (f ) explicitly. We will utilize (2.14) in Section 4 for numerical illustrations of the topological origin of the skin effect.
For the sake of completeness, we will devote the last part in this section to tridiagonal k-Laurent operators that are defined as
with σ det (f ) being defined by (2.11).

div.head: Eigenvectors of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators
div.p: Now that we have characterized the spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators, we will venture onwards to explore their eigenvectors. The following theorem is the second main result of this paper. Theorem 2.9. Suppose Π k j=1 c j ̸ = 0 and Π k j=1 b j ̸ = 0. Let f (z) ∈ C k×k be the symbol (2.5) and let λ ∈ C \σ ess (T (f )). If k j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) < 0 for λ j being the eigenvalues of f (z), then there exists an eigenvector x ∈ ℓ 2 of T (f ) associated to λ and some ρ < 1 such that
where C > 0 is a constant depending only on λ, a p , b p , c p for 1 ≤ p ≤ k.
Proof. Let λ ∈ C be such that
div.formula: k j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) = wind(det(f (T) -λ), 0) < 0. Since k i=1 b i ̸ = 0, k i=1 c i ̸ = 0, by Lemma A.1, det(f (z) -λ) is a meromorphic function in C
div.p: , with poles at 0 and ∞. Then the argument principle implies that det(f (z) -λ) = 0 for two values z 1 , z 2 with 1 < 1 ρ ≤ |z j | , j = 1, 2, counted with multiplicity. Moreover, since det(f (z i ) -λ) = 0, we find that λ is an eigenvalue to f (z i ) and hence there exists an associated eigenvector v i . Assume for now that z 1 ̸ = z 2 . Consider the vectors defined by

The vector u i satisfies the eigenvalue equation T (f )u i = λu i in all but the first row. Since u 1 , u 2 are linearly independent, there exists a linear combination of them that is indeed an eigenvector of T (f ).

Next, we consider the case where z 1 = z 2 and first suppose that dim Eig(f (z 1 ), λ) > 1, where Eig(f (z 1 ), λ) denotes the eigenspace of f (z 1 ) associated to the eigenvalue λ. Then there exist linearly independent eigenvectors v 1 , v 2 of f (z 1 ) associated to λ and we can use

to construct an exponentially decaying eigenvector of T (f ). Finally, it remains to treat the case when z 1 = z 2 and dim Eig(f (z 1 ), λ) = 1. Denote v 1 the eigenvector of f (z 1 ) associated to λ. We will construct a vector v 2 such that

satisfies the eigenvalue problem in all but the first row. Similarly to the previous arguments, this is enough to construct the eigenvector of T (f ). By explicitly writing down (T (f )λ)u 2 = 0 (except for the first row), we find that v 2 must satisfy the condition

which is also a sufficient condition. From Lemma A.1, it is not hard to see that for all λ in a small neighborhood of λ, the equation det(f (z) -λ) has two distinct roots. Let (ε j ) j be a sequence converging to zero. We define λ(ε j ) = λ + ε j . Then by the previous observation, we may assume that to each λ(ε j ) there exist two distinct roots z 1 (ε j ), z 2 (ε j ) of the equation det(f (z) -λ(ε j )) = 0. Let w 1 (ε j ), w 2 (ε j ) be the corresponding unit eigenvectors. We may assume that
div.formula: w 1 (ε j ) → v 0 , w 2 (ε j ) → v 0 .
div.p: (2.23) The limit of w i (ε j ) (or a subsequence thereof) exists by a standard compactness argument and it must be an eigenvector of f (z 1 ) associated to λ. In particular, for each j it holds that
div.formula: A 1 + (A 0 -λ(ε j ))z 1 (ε j ) -1 + A -1 z 1 (ε j ) -2 w 1 (ε j ) = 0, A 1 + (A 0 -λ(ε j ))z 2 (ε j ) -1 + A -1 z 2 (ε j ) -2 w 2 (ε j ) = 0. We define ∆z(ε j ) = z 1 (ε j ) -1 -z 2 (ε j ) -1 . Then we have A 1 w 1 (ε j ) -w 2 (ε j ) ∆z(ε j ) + (A 0 -λ(ε j )) z 1 (ε j ) -1 w 1 (ε j ) -z 2 (ε j ) -1 w 2 (ε j ) ∆z(ε j ) + A -1 z 1 (ε j ) -2 w 1 (ε j ) -z 2 (ε j ) -2 w 2 (ε j ) ∆z(ε j ) = 0.

div.head: Further expansion of this expression then yields
div.p: (2.24) Consider now the sequence w1(εj )-w2(εj ) ∆z(εj )

. If a bounded subsequence exists, we may define v 2 as the limit of this subsequence. In this case, it is not hard to see that v 2 satisfies the condition (2.22). Otherwise, there exists a subsequence of w1(εj )-w2(εj ) ∆z(εj )
whose norm tends to infinity. Define ∆w(ε j ) = ∥w 1 (ε j ) -w 2 (ε j )∥.
Then (upon passing to said subsequence) it holds that ∆z(ε j ) ∆w(ε j ) → 0.
(2.25) Multiplying (2.24) with this yields
In the limit, the right-hand side tends to 0. Thus v 2 = lim εj →0 w1(εj )-w2(εj ) ∆w(εj )
satisfies

This indicates that v 2 is an eigenvector. But from the construction of v 2 , it becomes apparent that v 2 must be orthogonal to v 1 , which contradicts the assumption that the eigenspace of f (z 1 ) associated with λ is one-dimensional. Therefore, the sequence
is uniformly bounded and arguments for the first case already demonstrate the existence of such v 2 satisfying (2.22).

The exponential bound in (2.18) is clear by the construction of the vectors u 1 and u 2 . ■ Remark 2.10. Note that the constructive approach used in the proof of [21,Theorem 7.2] does not work here. We have used a new approximation approach to construct the eigenvectors.

Remark 2.11. For the case when k j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) > 0, λ ∈ σ(T (f )) is because T (f ) has a non-dense image. In particular, one can construct the eigenvector x of T (f ) ⊤ -λ in the same spirit of Theorem 2.9, which implies T (f ) -λ has a non-dense image.

div.head: Pseudo-spectra of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices
div.p: In this section, we will apply the results of the previous section to matrices. As already described by Trefethen in [20], the spectrum of non-Hermitian matrices is very sensitive to small perturbations. For this reason, it is often advisable to study their pseudo-spectra, rather than their spectra. For the convenience of the reader, we recall the definition of pseudoeigenvalues and pseudoeigenvectors.
div.formula: Definition 3.1. Let ε > 0. Then, λ ∈ C is an ε-pseudoeigenvalue of A ∈ C N ×N if one of the following conditions is satisfied: (i) λ is a proper eigenvalue of A + E for some E ∈ C N ×N such that ∥E∥ ≤ ε; (ii) ∥(A -λI)u∥ < ε for some vector u with ∥u∥ = 1; (iii) ∥(A -λI) -1 ∥ -1 ≤ ε.
div.p: The set of all ε-pseudoeigenvalues of A, the ε-pseudospectrum, is denoted by σ ε (A). If some nonzero u satisfies ∥(λ -A)u∥ < ε, then we say that u is an ε-pseudoeigenvector of A.
By the equivalence of norms in finite dimensions, any norm can be used. For a general treatment of pseudospectra, see [21].

We consider the family of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices {A N } of various dimensions obtained as N ×N finite sections of the infinite operator T (f ). Results on the pseudospectra of the family of Toeplitz matrices can be found in [20]. On the other hand, the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices is fully understood and closed form characteristic polynomials have been found in [13].
Let us also introduce the sets
where T r = {z ∈ C : |z| = r} and let σ ε (A N ) be the set of ε-pseudoeigenvalues of A N .

The connection between the eigenvectors of a tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator and those of the associated tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrix can be made with an argument similar to [21,Theorem 7.2]. The first N components of an eigenvector of the Toeplitz operator can be used as the ε-pseudoeigenvector of the tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrix A N . Since we have proven the existence of an exponentially decaying eigenvector in Theorem 2.9, we can conclude with the following statement on tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices.

div.head: Theorem 3.2. Let {A N } be a family of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices such that
div.formula: k i=1 b i ̸ = 0, k i=1 c i ̸ = 0.
div.p: Then, for any r < 1 and ρ > r, we have
div.formula: Ω r ∪ Ω 1/r ∪ (σ(T (f )) + ∆ ε ) ⊆ σ ε (A N ) for ε = max(C 1 , ⌈N/k⌉C 2 )ρ ⌈N/k⌉-1 , (3.3)
div.p: where ∆ ε is a closed unit disk of radius ε and C 1 , C 2 are constants depending on r, ρ, and a j , b j , c j for 1 ≤ j ≤ k but independent of N . In particular, for the corresponding λ ∈ Ω r ∪ Ω 1/r , there exist nonzero pseudoeigenvectors v (N ) satisfying
div.formula: ∥(A N -λ)v (N ) ∥ ∥v (N ) ∥ ≤ max(C 3 , ⌈N/k⌉C 4 )ρ ⌈N/k⌉-1 (3.4) such that |v (N ) j | max i |v (N ) i | ≤ C 5 ⌈N/k⌉ρ ⌈j/k⌉-1 , if λ ∈ Ω 1/r , C 5 ⌈N/k⌉ρ ⌈(N -j)/k⌉-1 , if λ ∈ Ω r , 1 ≤ j ≤ N,(3.5)
div.p: where C 3 , C 4 , C 5 are constants independent of N .

Proof. Firstly, we note that the inclusion σ(T (f )) + ∆ ε ⊆ σ ε (A N ) is triviality valid for any matrix or operator. Secondly, by symmetry, Ω r must satisfy an estimate of the type (3.3) if Ω 1/r does. Thus all that we have to prove is Ω 1/r ⊆ σ ε (A N ). Given any r < 1, let λ ∈ Ω 1/r be arbitrary. Since wind(det(f (T 1 r )-λ), 0) < 0, similarly to the arguments in the proof of Theorem 2.9, we can show that det(f (z)-λ) = 0 for two values z 1 , z 2 with 1 < 1 ρ ≤ |z j | , j = 1, 2, counted with multiplicity. Also, since det(f (z i ) -λ) = 0, we find that λ is an eigenvalue to f (z i ) and hence there exists an associated eigenvector v i . Assume for now that z 1 ̸ = z 2 . Consider the vectors defined by

where without loss of generality we assume that N is divisible by k. This vector satisfies the eigenvalue equation A N u i = λu i in all but the first and last rows. Since u 1 , u 2 are linearly independent, there exists a normalized linear combination v (N ) = a 1 u 1 + a 2 u 2 so that (A N -λ)v (N ) = 0 in all but the last row. It is not hard to see that
where C 3 depends on a j , b j , c j , λ but not on N . Then since 1
div.formula: < 1 ρ ≤ |z j | , j = 1, 2, we obtain the bound ∥(A N -λ)v (N ) ∥ ≤ C 3 ρ ⌈N/k⌉-1 , (3.8)
div.p: which proves the claim (3.4) if z 1 ̸ = z 2 . If the roots z 1 , z 2 are distinct for all λ ∈ Ω 1/r , then the function sup
is a continuous function of λ on the compact set Ω 1/r ; its maximum provides the constant C 1 in (3.3) that independent of λ, N , and we can take ρ = r.
In the case where z 1 = z 2 , one can resort to a similar argument as the one in Theorem 2.9 to utilize

(3.9) to construct the pseudoeigenvector. This yields a similar bound except with C 3 ρ ⌈N/k⌉-1 in (3.4) replaced by an algebraically growing factor at worst ⌈N/k⌉C 4 ρ ⌈N/k⌉-1 . Then since the function sup
is a continuous function of λ on the compact set Ω 1/r , its maximum provides the constant C 2 in (3.3). Finally, (3.5) is deduced from construction (3.6) and (3.9). ■

div.head: Topological origin of the skin effect in polymer systems of subwavelength resonators
div.p: The non-Hermitian skin effect is the phenomenon whereby in the subwavelength regime a large proportion of the bulk eigenmodes of a non-Hermitian open system of subwavelength resonators are localised at one edge of the structure [8,19]. It has been realized experimentally in topological photonics, phononics, and other condensed matter systems [14,17,19]. Its significance lies in its substantial contribution to advancing the realm of active metamaterials, paving the way for novel opportunities to guide and control energy on subwavelength scales [3,6,7,12].

Recently, the non-Hermitian skin effect has been demonstrated in one-dimensional systems of subwavelength resonators using first-principle mathematical analysis. In particular, [1] demonstrates the skin effect for the monomer case when an imaginary gauge potential is introduced inside the resonators to break Hermiticity and [5] generalizes the results in [1] to dimer systems. We also refer to [2] for the skin effect in a three-dimensional setting and to [4] for stability analysis.

By the results obtained in the previous sections, we provide the topological origin of the skin effect in the one-dimensional polymer system, i.e., in systems with periodically repeated cells of k resonators. This also elucidates the numerical findings in [5] for the topological origin of the skin effect.
Let us first introduce the model setting. We consider a chain of N disjoint one-dimensional resonators
div.formula: D := (x L i , x R i ), where (x L,R i ) 1≤i≤N ⊂ R are the 2N extremities such that x L i < x R i < x L i+1 for any 1 ≤ i ≤ N .
div.p: The lengths of the i-th resonators will be denoted by
div.formula: ℓ i = x R i -x L
div.p: i and the spacings between the i-th and the (i + 1)-th resonators will be denoted by s i = x L i+1 -x R i . Furthermore, we assume that k resonators are periodically repeated, that is, s i+k = s i . The resulting system is illustrated in Figure 4.1. Let D be the domain consisting of the union of all resonators The underlying wave equation that governs the system is given by
div.formula: D = N i=1 (x L i , x R i ) ⊂ R. (4.1) x L 1 x R 1 ℓ 1 x L 2 x R 2 ℓ 2 x L 3 x R 3 ℓ 3 . . . x L k x R k ℓ k x L k+1 x R k+1 ℓ 1 . . . x L N -2 x R N -2 ℓ k-2 x L N -1 x R N -1 ℓ k-1 x L N x R N ℓ k s 1 s 2 s k s k-2 s k-1

                     u ′′ (x) + γu ′ (x) + ω 2 v 2 u = 0, x ∈ D, u ′′ (x) + ω 2 v 2 b u = 0, x ∈ R\D, u| R x L,R i -u| L x L,R i = 0, for all 1 ≤ i ≤ N, du dx R x L i = δ du dx L x L i , for all 1 ≤ i ≤ N, du dx L x R i = δ du dx R x R i , for all 1 ≤ i ≤ N, du d|x| -i ω v u = 0, at x = x L 1 , x R N , respectively,(4.2)
div.p: where 0 < δ ≪ 1, the wave speeds inside the resonator and in the background are respectively v b and v and are of order one, and γu ′ for γ ∈ R * denotes the imaginary gauge potential.

Here, for a function w we denote by w| L (x) := lim if the limits exist. We are interested in nontrivial frequencies ω such that (4.2) is satisfied and ω → 0 as δ → 0. Such ω are called subwavelength resonances.
In the high-contrast, low-frequency setting described above, gauge capacitance matrices provide a discrete approximation of the subwavelength eigenfrequencies induced in the structure.
Definition 4.1. For γ ∈ R * , we define the gauge capacitance matrix
div.formula: C γ ∈ R N ×N by C γ i,j =                  γ s1 ℓ1 1-e -γℓ 1 , i = j = 1, γ si ℓi 1-e -γℓ i -γ si-1 ℓi 1-e γℓ i , 1 < i = j < N, -γ si ℓi 1-e -γℓ j , 1 ≤ i = j -1 ≤ N -1, γ sj ℓi 1-e γℓ j , 2 < i = j + 1 ≤ N, -γ s N -1 ℓ N 1-e γℓ N , i = j = N, ,(4.3)
div.p: and all the other entries are zero.

In [1, Corollary 2.6], one can find a formal argumentation that the gauge capacitance matrix gives indeed a valid approximation of the eigenmodes in a subwavelength regime as δ → 0. For the remainder of this paper, we assume that γ = ℓ i = 1. Without loss of generality, we consider a system of nk resonators and obtain a gauge capacitance matrix of the tridiagonal k-Toeplitz form (perturbed):
For the semi-infinite systems we obtain a perturbed tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operator

with f being the symbol (2.5). It has been observed and conjectured [5] that the exponential decay of the eigenvectors of k-Toeplitz matrices for k ≥ 2 is due to the winding of the eigenvalues of the symbol being nontrivial. Now, by the spectral theory for the tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators introduced in the previous sections, we have the following results for the topological origin of the skin effect in subwavelength resonator systems, validating the conjecture made in [5]. Theorem 4.2. Suppose Π k j=1 c j ̸ = 0 and Π k j=1 b j ̸ = 0. Let f (z) ∈ C k×k be the symbol (2.5) and let λ ∈ C \σ ess (T a (f )). If k j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) < 0, then there exists an eigenvector x of T a (f ) associated to λ and some ρ < 1 such that
where
div.formula: C > 0 is a constant depending only on λ, a j , b j , c j , j = 1, • • • , k. If k j=1
div.p: wind(λ j (T), λ) > 0, then the above results hold for the left eigenvectors.

Proof. Although with a perturbation a on the first element, the proof of Theorem 2.9 can still be applied. ■ Theorem 4.2 elucidates the topological origin of the skin effect in the polymer system of subwavelength resonators. In particular, the skin effect holds for all λ in the region This is a generalization for the topological origin of the skin effect in the Toeplitz operator case given in [1]. The last part of the section is devoted to illustrating numerically the skin effect and its topological origin in chains of 2 and 3 periodically repeated resonators. We start by illustrating in Figure 4.2 the results of a system of 2 periodically repeated resonators as in [5]. In Figure 4.2A, we show the spectrum and pseudospectrum of the gauge capacitance matrix of a system of 25 dimers together with the winding of the two eigenvalues of the symbol of the corresponding 2-Toeplitz operator. Figure 4.2B shows that all the eigenvectors (black eigenvectors) associated with eigenvalues inside the region G in (4.6) are localised and the only non-decreasing eigenvector (gray eigenvector) corresponds to the eigenvalue 0 in the boundary of the region G. On the other hand, in [5] it is observed and conjectured that the non-trivial winding of the eigenvalues λ j (z) predicts the exponential decay of the eigenmodes. This is due to wind(λ j (T), λ) ≤ 0, j = 1, 2 in the example, which yields

figure.figDesc: Chapter 23, Theorem 4.3]. Its counterpart for Toeplitz operators was already established in [15, Chapter 23, Corollary 4.4].

figure.head: Theorem 2 . 8 .
figure.figDesc: ) with the generating symbol f being (2.5). Using the identification L(f -λ) = L(f ) -λ, which we have used to characterize the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators, one can obtain an explicit characterization of the spectrum of tridiagonal k-Laurent operators as a consequence of [15, Chapter 23, Corollary 2.5]. The following theorem holds. Let f ∈ (L(T) ∞ ) k×k and denote the associated tridiagonal k-Laurent operator L(f ). Then, it holds that

figure.head: Figure 4 . 1 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 4.1. A chain of N times periodically repeated k subwavelength resonators, with lengths (ℓ i ) 1≤i≤N and spacings (s i ) 1≤i≤N -1 .
-s) and w| R (x) := lim s→0,s>0 w(x + s)

figure.head: Figure 4 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 4.3 illustrates the results for 3 periodically repeated resonators. We numerically verify that indeed all the eigenvectors, except the one associated with eigenvalue 0 on the boundary of G, are localised at the left edge of the structure.
Computation performed for s1 = 1, s2 = 2, s3 = 3, and N = 50. Simulation with s1 = 1, s2 = 2, s3 = 3, and N = 50. Computation performed for s1 = 2, s2 = 3, s3 = 4, and N = 50.
Simulation with s1 = 2, s2 = 3, s3 = 4, and N = 50.

figure.head: Figure 4 . 3 . 1 z - 1 + (- 1 ) k+1 c k c k- 1 c 1 a 2
figure.figDesc: Figure 4.3. Figures A and C show the spectrum of the operator. The green regions consist of all the eigenvalues λ that satisfy 3 j=1 wind(λ j (T), λ) ̸ = 0. The black dots along the real line denote the spectrum of the gauge capacitance matrix C γ and the solid blue and orange lines around the spectrum are the ε-pseudospectra for ε = 10 k and k = -5, -2. Figures B and D show the eigenvectors of C γ .Proof. The case when k = 1, 2 is easy to verify. For the case when k ≥ 3, by repeated Laplace expansion, one can show that
Definition 2.1. The Hardy spaces H 2 := H 2 (T) and H 2

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: This work was partially supported by Swiss National Science Foundation grant number 200021-200307.

div.head: Code Availability
div.p: The data that support the findings of this work are openly available at https://doi. org/10.5281/zenodo.10438679.

Remark 4.3. We remark that the green regions for k = 1, 2, 3, 4 can be computed analytically through the formula (2.14). For the case when k ≥ 5, one can utilize (2.14) to compute numerically the green region.

div.head: Concluding remarks
div.p: We have developed new theories of spectra and pseudo-spectra for tridiagonal k-Toeplitz operators and matrices. Specifically, we have established the relationship between the winding number of the symbol function's eigenvalues and the exponential decay property of the corresponding eigenvectors (or pseudo-eigenvectors). This discovery sheds light on the topological origin of the non-Hermitian skin effect observed in one-dimensional polymer systems of subwavelength resonators. This paper also opens the door to the study of the spectral theory of block Toeplitz operators.

div.head: Conflict of interest
div.p: The authors have no competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article.

div.head: Appendix A. Computation of the determinant
div.p: Lemma A.1. For λ ∈ C \σ ess (T (f )), the determinant det(f (z) -λ), where f is the symbol of the tridiagonal Toeplitz operator T given in (2.5), has the form
where g is a polynomial in λ of degree k, defined by (A.2). In particular, there are at most 2k λ ∈ C so that det(f (z) -λ) = 0 admits a double root.
To demonstrate the last claim, we write

By multiplication with z, we obtain that z det(f (z) -λ) = 2 + B + g(λ)z. Thus only when g(λ) 2 -4AB = 0, we have a double root. Since g(λ) is a polynomial of order k, we have at most 2k solutions λ of the above equation so that the root is a double root. ■

monogr.title: Mathematical foundations of the non-Hermitian skin effect
The non-Hermitian skin effect in three dimensions

analytic.title: Edge Modes in Subwavelength Resonators in One Dimension

monogr.title: Stability of the non-Hermitian skin effect
Perturbed Block Toeplitz matrices and the non-Hermitian skin effect in dimer systems of subwavelength resonators
Functional Analytic Methods for Discrete Approximations of Subwavelength Resonator Systems

analytic.title: Topologically Protected Edge Modes in One-Dimensional Chains of Subwavelength Resonators

monogr.title: Journal de Mathématiques Pures et Appliquées
Introduction to Large Truncated Toeplitz Matrices
Banach Algebra Techniques in Operator Theory
Subwavelength Resonant Acoustic Scattering in Fast Time-Modulated Media

analytic.title: Subwavelength Resonances in One-Dimensional High-Contrast Acoustic Media
The characteristic polynomial of some perturbed tridiagonal k-Toeplitz matrices
Observation of Non-Hermitian Topology and Its Bulk-Edge Correspondence in an Active Mechanical Metamaterial

monogr.title: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

analytic.title: Localization Transitions in Non-Hermitian Quantum Mechanics
Non-Hermitian skin effect beyond the tight-binding models
Spectra and pseudospectra of block Toeplitz matrices
Topological Origin of Non-Hermitian Skin Effects
Eigenvalues and pseudo-eigenvalues of Toeplitz matrices
